speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Mr. David Mandel, and this is the fourth tape. Mr. Mandel, you were just telling us how you were reunited with your father and that he was very, very thin, and how you traded on the black market for insulin for him. Then what happened? How did you reunite with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as-- after-- as he was gaining strength, he talked about let's gain strength, and we'll go back to Germany and look for the rest of the family. By that time, I knew that the only chance we had for the rest of the family-- immediate family-- was my brother. I already knew that anyone who went to the right when we were separated, they all ended up going to the crematorium, and no one survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we-- I hoped that for my brother, not knowing his whereabouts and what happened to him-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so we held out hope for my brother and other parts of the family. We spent quite a few-- a couple of months-- in Krakow, because the area where we come from, are Carpathians. And Czechoslovakia was not, as yet, liberated. The war hasn't ended yet. And after my father gained strength, we left Krakow. We went back to Munkacs, and we found the city half empty. All those people-- most of them never came back. A few survivors, stragglers, would come back. We set up housekeeping, waiting for members of the family to come back. And then the end of May of 1945, we got word that my brother survived. My brother was liberated by the Americans, the end of the war, on May 5, in West Germany. He marched them all the way to the other side, and he was liberated on May 5, on his birthday. So he has a way of saying that he was born twice. And he was 18 years old when he was liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we waited. And he came home. And the three of us, when we were convinced that there's no one around, our existence in Munkacs would have been a very unhappy one. And we did have an aunt living in the United States, and we-- we decided, you know, that we will somehow get in touch with her. So we left Munkacs, and we went to Czechoslovakia. That's Moravia. And we lived in a city called Teplice-Sanov, not too far from Prague, where we lived with some cousins, also survivors, two brothers and two sisters. And we lived there for a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that, we decided to make arrangements, maybe, if we have a chance, to migrate, emigrate, to the United States, because that's the only relative that we really have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] aside from Europe. So we went to Germany. We were placed in a displaced person camp, Gabersee, near Munich. And we were there. My father was put in a hospital. He was being treated for his sugar diabetic. They took care of him. He got the right amount of insulin, and slowly he just learned to live. And-- and so he got better. As time went on, he got stronger and stronger. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a rough life for my dad, especially, having lost his-- most of his family. I remember I noticed it more than ever. Started to notice it in the DP camp how, in the middle of the night, he would cry out. He took on the responsibility, that it was his fault. See, my sister, who was five years old, there was a Gentile family who loved her very much. And they begged my father [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to have her stay with them. And at the time, my father thought about it, but he came to the decision, you know, that families have to stick together. No normal person would have thought at the time that, in the 20th century, they would kill men, women, and even little children. Also, the fact that he sent my younger brother back to the mother, trying to help out my mother and his younger children, that he sent him to his death. My father lived for 30 day-- 30 years after-- after having survived the Nazi death camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in DP camp, we were there, and we were prepared-- we were preparing ourselves to start a new life. And we were lucky to have come to the United States. On July 15, 1946, we arrived in New York. And the [INAUDIBLE] helped us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came-- then after that, we came to Pittsburgh. And we lived with our aunt. My father got a job as a carpenter. I got a job, and I went to night school to learn English. My brother went to continue on as a dental technician. And we started a-- a new life. My father was matched up with a lady in Detroit who husband passed on. And so we eventually moved to Detroit. My father remarried to a wonderful lady who comes from the same part of the world as we do, but she lived here for some 30-odd years before she had her own children. And also a very-- comes from a very religious home, and my father was religious. He was a [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] God-fearing, very pious man. And he-- she took very good care of him. And she became our second mother. And I met a-- my brother married, remarried-- married. And he's got children of his own. And somehow, I never married. And I moved eventually to Grand Rapids, Michigan. I went to work for a man in a men's store-- I remember, 1948-- for $25 a week. Eventually became his partner. And-- and then I bought him out. And I enjoyed working. I kept busy. I kept my mind occupied. I took up golf. And I keep busy. I'm very hyper person. I've got to keep going all the time. And I'm retired now to Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think your experience during your growing up time and what you-- how you survived has affected your relationships and how you are today? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I am committed now. It was-- for the first 35 years, I didn't speak about the Holocaust. But I'm committed to bear witness what happened for all those who died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the fact that there are people, deniers, revisionists, who claim that the whole thing is a hoax, and they're doing it while survivors are still alive, I have to speak up. Even though every time I speak, it pains me. And I've made up my mind to speak to the youth, the future generations. They want to know. They are our future historians. They're our future voters. They're our future leaders. It's important that they have to know about the Holocaust and they have to study the Holocaust. The Holocaust is part of history. It's important to mankind to know what took place. God forbid it should never happen again. Maybe by being a witness, they can become witnesses to avert a Holocaust like this of ever happening again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned earlier on in the tape that you did not have an opportunity to have a bar mitzvah. Is this something you might consider in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about it a lot. And now that I'm settled and I'm retired, I'm seriously thinking of one of these days to give in. My family wants me to, and I'm beginning to feel very strongly about it. And one of these days, I will have my bar mitzvah finally.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your final message to our future generations? To-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] to-- to be active, to take part, and not to stand by and let the world go on. They should-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they should be aware that this Holocaust can take place again and try to avert of anything like this happening again. Mr. Mandel, on behalf of the Shoah Foundation, and on behalf of myself, I want to thank you from the bottom of my heart for giving this testimony, because without you, this wouldn't be possible. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. A-9328. This is a picture of my immediate family. From left to right is my younger brother, Milo. And next to him, that's me and my mother to the right. And down below is my sister, Vera. And my older brother, Morris. And then my brother, and my father, and my youngest brother, Yehuda. And this picture was taken in 1940, already when we were under the influence of the Nazis. This is a picture of my aunt's wedding, taken in 1935. One of the few pictures that I have of my family other than immediate family. On the very end, on the left side, that's my grandmother, Rifka, who is my mother's mother. And in the middle is my mother's sister, Dora, with her husband, Avram. My mother is on the other end with my father, who's holding my younger brothers-- my younger brother. And in the front, there's my oldest brother, who was just about seven years old. I happen to remember that wedding, even though I was about five or six years old. And those-- all those people-- all of them-- have been murdered in the death camps during the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Munkacs, Czechoslovakia, where all my family lived at one time. This is a picture of my parent's engagement. My father and my mother. It was probably taken in 1926. I found this picture at my aunt's. That's one of the few pictures that I have of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she engaged? In what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munkacs. This is my brother next to me. Out of five brothers, he was the third one. Shmuel, with the violin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was about maybe 13 years old when that picture was taken. And that was taken right in the ghetto, and those are-- that's one of the pictures that I smuggled through concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year do you think it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in 19-- early 1944 in Munkacs. This is a picture of the City of Munkacs, where my family lived for generations and where I lived for the first 14 years of my life. That is the main street. Every time I look at it, it's just like it was yesterday. This picture was taken in 1947 in Detroit, where we, as refugees, survivors of camps, most of us, formed a soccer team called the Maccabees. I am the one on the left side right next to the manager. And next to me is Mike Vogel, the fellow who spent two and a half years in Birkenau-Auschwitz, and also survivor. And there's my brother-- one, two, three, four, five, six-- seventh to the right. And that is a soccer team that we were forming a new life in this country here. This is a picture of my brother, who is also a survivor, his grandchildren-- Yitzy, Yehuda, and Jaffa. The offspring of a survivor. Beautiful kids. This is a picture of my brother's daughter, Sherry, her husband, David, and the two kids. In the center, the little one is Kiva, and his brother Noah. And since this picture's been taken, they have a third one whose name is Ezra. They live in Cleveland, Ohio. Very proud of my new family.
